speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four of the interview with Greta Klingsberg, formerly Hofmeister. OK, so you were telling us about Paris on the way to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so Palestine was the first [BACKGROUND NOISE] smell really of independent and freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paris, you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris, of course. Yes, Paris. Where we could buy a book, and where we could go around wherever and whenever we wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we, again, gathered to Marseilles and entering a ship is a lot of other Jewish people, more or less grown up, to go to Palestine. When we arrived in Palestine-- it was a very nice ship. It was called [INAUDIBLE], and we had food and there we had-- we had a lot of fun. When we came to Palestine there was a strike. I couldn't even mention that my-- to my parents that we arrived. And they didn't know either, because there was-- this was a mandate of the British. And I don't know why, but it was a strike. Anyway, it took a few days until my parents came to-- I think it was Kiryat Bialik or something next to Haifa where we were, unfortunately again, in some kind of a camp to-- to wait to be dispersed. So I waited. And all of a sudden, there was-- I mean, a pair of people in the city that are your parents. It was quite something to live through and to get to know you and love. It takes time, and it is not easy. It's a not a process that goes from one day to the other. And my [INAUDIBLE] I also remember that my father didn't allow me to read certain books, which I couldn't understand. I didn't understand it. Somebody is going to tell me not to read something. Of course, immediately I took it, went to the toilet, and started to read it, not understanding. Because it was-- I think it was by Boccaccio. And I had no idea about sex or anything, because this was the farthest thing away from us, even 16-year-old of these times who grew up all together. And it was always friends, friends, friends and nothing else, of course, but very intense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father used to also check up on me every night, because I was not reading. I was swallowing books. I was so hungry to-- to be like anybody, and be-- I always had this fear that because I never went to school, I must be terribly uneducated and stupid. Which I found out that it's not true, but it took some time. And of course, I could not afford to go to school here, because I had to start to work. My parents didn't have any money. What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to work in an English camp as a foreman. My mother used to work-- whatever they found, whether it was picking oranges or I don't know exactly. Anyway, I ran away to Jerusalem, because I wanted to study music. And that's the only thing, and they always told me the answer of even the youth-- or the youth-- I don't know what kind of organization it was then-- said we don't need musicians. Very nice to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: But for me it was, OK, you don't? I will go through, and I did. And I got a stipend, and I studied in music as long as it was possible until, of course, there was-- the war broke out. And then I was already a very firm Jerusalemite not willing to go back to my parents who lived in Hadera, which was very bad for my parents maybe, but I would never leave Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was also injured in Jerusalem. A bomb exploded in front of me, but nothing much happened. I only was very scared because I thought I'd never walk again. And there was no electricity at that time in Jerusalem. There was no food-- that really didn't matter-- and there was almost no water. That was worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which area did you live in in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wherever I found place, because I-- people, we away from Jerusalem. I was-- at the time, I was living with a person who was divorced, and the took-- taking care of the child and cleaning the house and even cooking, which was very funny because I had no idea how to cook. But she had to work, this lady, and she sent people-- she sent some food home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to phone-- I mean, I used to ring my neighbor and say, look, there is a piece of meat. What am I going to do with? And slowly I learned how to do it, and she was not so very-- I mean, she took whatever she could, and it was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the afternoon, I studied with [INAUDIBLE] and Alexander and all different people in Palestine-- Israel not yet, but-- and that's it until after the war. Again, we had to start to work, so I started in the music shop and different kind of jobs. Always studying until I ended in '56, '57 at the radio where that was a really fantastic job for me, because I was really responsible for ordering all the records and cataloging-- and catalog of foreign languages in music. Always somehow connected with music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at least I stayed faithful to where I started out. Being a child, I was always connected in music. Also-- actually because I sang in the Ankor choir of Jerusalem, and we had a lot of good performances also [INAUDIBLE]. We have-- in the name of Israel got prizes even in Vienna. Just imagine the music, the city. And it was-- I love to live and music helped me, and, of course, friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very lucky in finding the person who was the most important in my life who was my husband. Unfortunately, who isn't anymore. He was more than a partner, He was more than 20 years older than me, and he was my teacher, my-- my everything. My partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Reuven Klingsberg, and he was working at University in manuscripts. He was once-- once I thought that every Jew is an educated, interested, open, tolerant person. Well, he was. And he was a friend also to a lot of-- he also made a lot of exhibitions of Kafka and Freud as a Jew in the Hebrew University. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, that is a different story. That's-- because I was married when I was 18, because I thought that you get married if you get to know somebody. I mean, times and everything changed so fast, even in Israel, so it's very hard to talk about it. Anyway, this didn't take long, because it was really a disaster. It was not honest, and so we divorced. And it took me a long time to do-- to really marry, I mean, with all the legal and religious law. So we lived together not in one flat, but we were partners. And I mean, he remembered-- he helped me through thick and thin until we really decided to marry. And that's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you want to know exactly the year. It's OK. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is really-- in this case, it is quite unimportant, but it was-- because we were known as a couple and never-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] there was never any question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just like a legal--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was to legalize it, and it was quite complicated to legalize anything in Israel. But it was in '70 something. Yeah, '70 something. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we end--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I'd just like to give you a minute to say basically any closing words you'd like to say. Just a-- is there any message you would want to give to anyone who sees this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I gave a message-- a message is too strong a word, because I'm not a message giver. I only when-- when I spoke to the youth of Israel or even of Germany, when they asked me to say what message do I have to say, so I say, please leave open something to be curious at least. You don't have to accept what you see, but-- but at least get to know it before you condemn it. And if-- you don't have to become-- it is so much more interested to be interested in the other-- other person than to be tolerant. Because if you are not, you don't know what you are capable of if you run only one way, the right way. It is not so. I mean, God created so many differences in people, so accept it or just get to know it. And then maybe you can even accept it without becoming the other one. That's more or less, I would say. I don't know whether I would call it a message, but what I try to become-- that's why, as I told you before, that's why I speak also Arabic, which is something quite hard in this country. But that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are welcome. Well, this is a picture of my grandfather, my father's father, whom I personally never knew. But he was an actor, and this is an actor's print.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold Hofmeister. He even signed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, the name-- the full name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and where it was taken, when it was taken if you know. If you don't know, don't say. Or guess. It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of my mother as yet not married. She is called Paula Steiner, and she was the first Jewish woman who worked officially in a [GERMAN] which is a drugstore, selling newspapers and cigars and cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Vienna, and-- yeah. And I don't know exactly the year, but it is like, I think, about 1908, maybe 19-- I don't know. This is a picture of my father, Alfred Hofmeister, who is a volunteer in the first World War. So it must be between-- somewhere in between the first World War. This is a picture of my sister and me. On the left side is Trude, and I am the bigger one on the right side. And this is a picture of '35 still in Vienna under happier circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Trude Hofmeister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As we said before, Trude and Greta. Yes. This is my last school year. I guess it is '37. And I'm in the third row with my crossed legs outside. This is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is in Vienna?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- it's in Vienna in my second class of the public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1937 or 8, as I told before. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to mention some names of people, specific people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. One of my sister the fly. we're not going to get rid of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're on. We're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're cutting off-- it doesn't matter because I won't mention it, but can I show you anyway?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're recording.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the last pictures that we sent to our parents to Palestine. It's from the Jewish orphanage in Birneau. And next to the older lady who is the director of the orphanage sits my sister with her arms crossed, and I behind her.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the arms crossed is only on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- '39, '40, '43-- '42 maximum. I cannot remember-- this is impossible. It must be '42, because we already 11 and-- it-- it looks as if we were 11 and 12 more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's Trude and Greta Hofmeister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It is my sister Trude, and me, Greta on the left of the directors of the orphanage. This is definitely the last picture that we sent to Palestine to our parents, and this is-- on the left it's me, and-- Greta-- and on the right, my smaller sister, Trude Hofmeister. And it must have been at '42 before we left for Terezin. Still in the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And four corners. [INAUDIBLE] four corners.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is really-- you can-- it will not show it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but afterwards [? he's gonna ?] go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm recording.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. .

speaker: interviewee
text: So here you have the original-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can-- there's the frame of the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. So here's part of the picture of the participants of the children's opera. You have the boy in the background-- the taller one is Pepicek, and I'm in front of him as Annika. Not exactly [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, what was his name? His real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Peter [? Millstein. ?] He did not survive. And this is a day or so before we left for Auschwitz, all of us. Because this was-- this is a picture taken from the film that was done for propaganda purposes and with the Red Cross. The picture, it was called the [GERMAN] the Fuhrer Gives the Jews a Town. And in the background, you see the original poster of Terezin of the Opera [INAUDIBLE]. That its a children's opera [? in ?] [? two ?] pictures, and that's it more or less what it says, and who did it and who directed it. And that the singing and dancing, the children of Terezin. Yes. This is an album leaf that I wrote for my friend [INAUDIBLE] who survived it. She's-- she is living in Prague, and she's a children's physician. And I translated it before. It's in Czech, of course, and it was written in Terezin. The date is on the leaf-- album leaf. It's the 19th of July, '42. This is another album leaf written in Terezin in '43, and it was dedicated to Eva Diamantova, a girl who lived in the same room that I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the drawings left behind in Terezin by my sister Trude who used to draw like I used to sing. And when we left for Auschwitz, she-- it was just by chance left behind. And after 40 years, I found it via Elena Makarova, who did an exhibition of children's drawings in [INAUDIBLE] in Jerusalem, where it was mentioned that there are also some pictures of Trude Hofmeister. And it took me a long time, another few years, via Czech bureaucracy, to find and get copies of eight drawings of my sister. So it was like a [? shalom ?] or [? regard ?] from my sister, and it's the only thing that I have left from her. This is another of the seven pictures that my sister Trude Hofmeister did in Terezin and I found.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Laura Shimkova, who was my teacher, mentor, foster mother, and actually savior of all my-- to come more or less normal out of the whole thing. And she also took care of a lot of children. And this picture was taken a day before I left for Palestine to meet my parents, and it was in the year '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's the picture on the day we left in the [? repatriation-- ?] whatever you call it. I don't know. Anyway, a train that leaves with all the fugitives-- or not fugitives--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refuges, that's right. [? Repatriation ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And it's Juda, Eva, Alex, and me. I am on the right inside, and it's on the 2nd of April, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, could you give their full names? Everybody's full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can try. It's Eva Furthova. It's Juda Bakwan. It's Alex Schachter and Greta Hofmeister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only document we got after the war from the Red Cross so we could enter and go via Paris and Marseilles to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the [INAUDIBLE] on the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is April '46 in Haifa just after my arrival. I'm with my parents, my mother, my father, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is all in the family Hofmeister, Paula, Alfred, and Greta. This is my husband Reuven Klingsberg and me, Greta. And we are happy together in Jerusalem.
